tour.title: Project Overview & Doctrine
tour.description: Start with README.md to grasp what TauGo is — a kernel that exposes a single public API, Kernel.Decide(ctx, Exchange) -> Decision, returning one of three regimes (Deterministe | Probabiliste | Refus). CLAUDE.md then makes the project's anti-objectives explicit: TauGo is not a framework, not an orchestrator, not an LLM wrapper, not a network service. Understanding what TauGo refuses to be is as important as understanding what it does.

tour.title: The Canonical Specification
tour.description: PRD.md is the single source of truth for every decision the kernel makes — the 8-step dispatch algorithm, the 5 invariants I1-I5, the 3 dimensions, the 7 anti-patterns, and the Clean Architecture étanchéité rules. Every section of the codebase traces back to a PRD section. Skim it now to know where to return when something looks arbitrary.

tour.title: The CLI Entry Points
tour.description: Two binaries live under cmd/. The `tau` CLI in cmd/tau/main.go reads an Exchange JSON from stdin and emits a Decision JSON — it wires app.NewDispatcher (which loads calibration.DefaultProfile per ADR P0-02) and routes to runDecide or runCalibrate. cmd/tau/calibrate.go implements the calibration sub-command that produces a byte-reproducible Profile from a JSONL corpus. The cmd/generate-corpus binary produces deterministic synthetic corpora across six branches (refus-frontiere, refus-I3, refus-I4, deterministe, probabiliste, hysteresis) for testing and calibration seeding.

tour.title: Application Wiring & the LLM Boundary
tour.description: internal/app/app.go is the composition root — NewDispatcher injects a concrete LLM client and loads calibration.DefaultProfile() so that the periméd-profile guard (anti-pattern #3) is active on the default CLI path. The bridge/llm package defines the narrow Client interface (ADR-0003) that is the only contact point between the kernel and any concrete LLM, plus a deterministic Stub used by every test that does not set TAUGO_LLM_BACKEND=real. This inversion is what keeps internal/tau/* free of any LLM import (anti-pattern #6).
tour.languageLesson: Go interfaces are satisfied implicitly — bridge/llm.Client declares the method set, and any type with matching methods (the stub, a future real backend) satisfies it without an `implements` keyword. This is what lets app/ inject concrete implementations while internal/tau/* depends only on the interface, never on a concrete LLM package.

tour.title: The Kernel Heart — τ Operator, Frontier & Diagnostics
tour.description: internal/tau/operator.go is the highest fan-in file in the project (48 incoming edges) — it defines the public types Exchange, Decision, Trace, Score, Regime and the Kernel.Decide entry point. Before any scoring happens, FrontierCheck (frontier.go) verifies that the exchange satisfies the four classical conditions simultaneously; if it does not, the kernel returns Refus with a canonical Diag* constant from diagnostics.go. This is anti-pattern #2: no bypass of FrontierCheck.Inside() is ever permitted.

tour.title: The Three Dimensions (D-SENS, D-AUTORITÉ, D-INVARIANT)
tour.description: Chapter III.8.4 of the monograph defines three orthogonal axes in [0,1]: D-SENS (where meaning is fixed), D-AUTORITÉ (reach of the delegation chain), D-INVARIANT (support of integration invariants). Each has four probes implemented in dimensions/{dsens,dauthority,dinvariant}.go and a shared Score type in score.go. These scores are ventilated into Decision.Trace per ADR-0008, which is what lets I4 detect silent ruptures by reading the per-dimension scores instead of a single conflated TauScore proxy.

tour.title: The Five Refutable Invariants I1-I5
tour.description: The kernel's epistemic discipline lives here: each invariant in invariants/i{1..5}_*.go encodes one falsifiable property from chap. III.8.5 — I1 conservation, I2 irreducibility, I3 Searle-1995 authority asymmetry (refus ontologique without AttestationInstitutionnelle), I4 coherence (s ≈ pendant ⟹ i ≈ pendant), I5 conjunctive composition. evaluator.go orchestrates them. Each one ships a fuzz target (fuzz_targets_test.go) running at ~1-9 million exec/s — the kernel is a kernel because every invariant is continuously trying to refute itself.
tour.languageLesson: Go fuzz testing (introduced in 1.18) is built into the standard `testing` package — a function named FuzzXxx(f *testing.F) seeds inputs via f.Add(...) then receives mutated arguments through f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, ...) {}). Run with `go test -fuzz=FuzzI1_... -fuzztime=30s`. TauGo turns each invariant into a fuzz target so that the property is asserted on millions of randomly mutated exchanges — refutation becomes a routine CI activity, not a theoretical aspiration.

tour.title: Orchestration — The 8-Step Dispatch
tour.description: internal/orchestration/dispatcher.go has the highest fan-out in the project (33 edges) — it is where the 8 steps of the dispatch algorithm (frontière, gardes D-AUTORITÉ, péremption, I4, scores ventilation, composite + hystérèse, invariants) execute in a non-arbitrary order spelled out in docs/algorithms/dispatch.md. Step 6 reads Profile.Weights to combine the ventilated dimension scores into the composite. Any change to the step order must update both the algorithm spec and the dispatcher in lockstep.

tour.title: Calibration, Drift & the Expired Profile Guard
tour.description: internal/calibration/profile.go defines a Profile whose date_revision is checked on every dispatch — once today > date_revision, every call returns Refus(DiagPeremptionProfile). That is anti-pattern #3, and ADR P0-02 makes app.NewDispatcher load calibration.DefaultProfile() so the guard is active on the CLI default path. calibrate.go implements the V1 grid-search calibration in milli-units with deterministic tie-breaking, producing a byte-identical Profile JSON. drift.go enforces the five PRD §11.4 drift criteria via CPU/LLM/corpus fingerprints — a drift detected here is what motivates re-calibration.

tour.title: The Empirical Bridge (AgentMeshKafka)
tour.description: internal/bridge/agentmeshkafka/adapter.go ingests live AgentMeshKafka traces and returns a neutral DTO (ADR-0005) — the bridge never leaks Kafka or domain vocabulary into the kernel; mapping happens in internal/app/agentmesh.go. classifier.go labels traces against the kernel's regimes. The I4-report.md document closes the loop: it is the empirical validation of the I4 coherence invariant, demonstrating that the theory survives contact with real workloads (the criterion for an invariant to remain non-refuted).

tour.title: Architecture Guards & Anti-patterns
tour.description: internal/arch_test.go encodes the 7 Clean-Architecture rules (since v0.1.1) as executable Go tests that walk the import graph — any tau/* → orchestration import, any LLM concrete in tau/*, any calibration → tau/* edge fails CI. internal/anti_patterns_test.go does the same for the 7 CLAUDE.md anti-patterns: forbids exported Predict*/Forecast* identifiers, asserts I3PerimptionLimite stays in the future, checks unmodeled observations surface in Trace. These two files are why architectural decisions in TauGo cannot quietly erode — every PR re-litigates them.

tour.title: End-to-End Verification & CI
tour.description: test/e2e/calibration_determinism_test.go verifies that calibration is byte-reproducible across runs — modification of the golden hash would require an ADR. test/e2e/e2e_trace_test.go asserts that Decision.Trace exposes the ventilated DSens/DAuthority/DInvariant scores (ADR-0008) at the public boundary. The Makefile orchestrates `make test` (race + cover), `make fuzz` (30s on I1-I5), `make coverage` (gate ≥ 90% on tau/*, ≥ 80% global), and `make build-reproducible` (byte-identical builds). The .github/workflows/ci.yml pipeline runs the same matrix on 3 OS for every PR — this is what closes the loop from doctrine to merged code.
tour.languageLesson: Go's `testing` package has no separate e2e harness — tests under test/e2e/ are plain `*_test.go` files in their own package, invoked by `go test ./test/e2e/...`. TestMain functions (in cmd/tau/main_test.go) rebuild the binary in a temp dir before sub-tests exec it with stdin/stdout pipes — the same approach used by FibGo. Combined with `-race` (CGO required) and `-cover`, this gives end-to-end coverage without leaving the standard toolchain.
